title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Quarto is an open-source scientific and technical publishing system created by Posit. It helps researchers move from scattered workflows to a more integrated way of writing, documenting, presenting, and sharing their work.
This workshop website is designed as a guided reference. You can follow it from top to bottom during a live session, or return to individual pages later when you need help with a specific output format.






NoteWorkshop idea



The core promise of Quarto is simple: write once in a plain text source file and render to multiple professional outputs such as HTML, PDF, DOCX, and slides.




This material is especially useful for:

early career researchers building their first durable workflow
experienced researchers who want fewer disconnected tools
teams that care about reproducibility, transparency, and reuse
anyone who wants websites, manuscripts, presentations, and notes to feel part of the same system

Basic familiarity with at least one language such as R, Python, or Julia can help, but it is not required for most of the lessons here.



By working through this site, participants will learn how to:

understand what Quarto is and where it fits relative to R Markdown
write basic content using Markdown and YAML
install Quarto and understand the main tooling options
use Quarto for lab notebooks, manuscripts, presentations, websites, and dashboards
explore more advanced tools such as extensions, templates, and brand.yml




The workshop is organized in three parts:

Start with the Foundations pages if Quarto is new to you.
Move into the Academic Use Cases to see how one tool supports different research outputs.
Finish with the Advanced Topics when you are ready to customize or scale your work.

Each use-case lesson has a separate guided exercise page. The lesson explains the workflow, and the exercise helps you practice it with an academic scenario.



Academic work usually produces many related outputs:

rough notes and exploratory documentation
figures and tables for papers
slides for talks
websites for projects, labs, or personal profiles
interactive communication for stakeholders or collaborators

Quarto makes these outputs easier to manage because the underlying authoring model stays consistent. That means less time relearning tools and more time focusing on the research itself.






TipSuggested path



If you are brand new, begin with What Is Quarto? and then continue to Markdown for Beginners.
